content.full_text:
122019

State of California

0-76-10-027

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
October 14, 1976

Mr. John T. Hay
Executive Vice President
California Chamber of Commerce
445 Capitol Mall, Suite 300
P.O. Box 1736
Sacramento, CA 95808

Re: Request for Opinion
    No. 76-017

Dear Mr. Hay:

    This letter is to advise you that at its October
meeting the Fair Political Practices Commission instructed the
staff to send you this letter in lieu of the formal opinion
which you requested (No. 76-017). Since accepting your opinion
request, legislation has been enacted and the Commission has
adopted regulations which are dispositive of the issues posed
in your request. Accordingly, the questions presented in your
letter no longer pose a substantial question of interpretation
of the Political Reform Act.

    You asked whether the Chamber of Commerce would be a
committee pursuant to Gov. Code Section 82013(a) by virtue of
its receipt and payment of funds in connection with the Com-
mittee for a 2/3rds Vote on Taxation's lawsuit challenging
certain provisions of the Political Reform Act. Throughout
1975 and 1976, the staff has advised persons supporting or
opposing the qualification of a measure who are proponents of
opponents of the measure that they will not be a committee within
the meaning of the Act unless the Commission so determined in
opinion or the Act was amended. The Act has now been amended
(SB 1362) and beginning in January, 1977, persons supporting
or opposing the qualification of a measure will be required to
file campaign statements. However, for the remainder of this
year, the interim advice of the staff will remain in effect
and such persons will not be deemed committees. Accordingly,
the Chamber of Commerce need not file campaign statements as a
Section 82013(a) committee disclosing the collection and dis-
bursement of funds relative to its support of the Committee
for a 2/3rds Vote on Taxation (hereinafter the 2/3rds Vote
Committee).

1,3410

Mr. John T. Hay
October 14, 1976
Page Two


The 2/3rds Vote Committee, however, will have to
disclose the receipt of the payments for legal services in
connection with the aforementioned lawsuit as in-kind contri-
butions. As the proponent of a measure the 2/3rds Vote Com-
mittee incurred reporting obligations identical to those imposed
upon a committee formed or existing primarily for political
purposes. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215. This means
that all payments "received by or made at the behest of" the
2/3rds Vote Committee were contributions to the Committee.
Pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(c), a contribution
is "made at the behest of" a committee when it is made under
the "control or at the direction" of the committee. In the
instant case, the 2/3rds Vote Committee, in conjunction with
the Chamber of Commerce, controlled and directed the course of
the lawsuit since, although the Chamber of Commerce assigned
responsibility for hiring and compensating the attorneys, both
organizations instructed the attorneys with respect to the
conduct of the action. Accordingly, the payments from the
legal defense fund to the attorneys were "made at the behest
of" the 2/3rds Vote Committee and were contributions to the
2/3rds Vote Committee within the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215.

In addition, we think the contributions were made by
the individual members of the Chamber of Commerce, not the
Chamber itself. When a person gives money to a multipurpose
organization such as the Chamber of Commerce, he makes a "con-
tribution" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act if
the donated funds are "earmarked" for political purposes; and
payment is earmarked for political purposes when the donor
knows or has reason to know that the funds will be used by the
recipient to make contributions or expenditures. 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18215(d). In the instant case, the persons who
donated funds to the legal defense fund established by the
Chamber of Commerce knew that the funds would be used by the
2/3rds Vote Committee for the purpose of acquiring more cir-
culation time. Under these circumstances, we think that each
donor's funds were "earmarked" and that such funds, therefore,
were contributions. Accordingly, the information required by
Gov. Code Section 84210(b) should be disclosed with respect to
each member of the Chamber of Commerce who contributed $50.00
or more to the legal defense fund. In addition, the informa-
tion required by Gov. Code Section 84302 should be disclosed
with respect to the Chamber of Commerce.

Finally, if a member of the Chamber of Commerce
already was a committee pursuant to Gov. Code Section 82013,

Mr. John T. Hay
October 14, 1976
Page Three


the amount of any contribution to the legal defense fund should
be disclosed on its campaign statement. However, as indicated
earlier, no organization became a committee by virtue of sup-
porting the qualification of a measure prior to
January 1, 1977, and thus no organization which was not already
a committee would have become a committee by virtue of contri-
butions to the legal defense fund. In addition, no information with respect to the Chamber of Commerce need be disclosed
on the contributor's statement since Gov. Code Section 84302
imposes this obligation only on the recipient.

In closing, we note that persons who have filed
campaign statements previously which do not comport with the
reporting standards outlined above should amend their statements accordingly. If you have any further questions, please
contact Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission's Legal
Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:TP:plh
